[[af:Metaalaap (astrologie)]][[cdo:Gĕng-sĭng]][[de:Metall-Affe]][[en:Metal Monkey]][[fr:Singe de métal]][[hak:Kâng-sṳ̂n]][[ja:庚申]][[ko:경신]][[vi:Canh Thân]][[za:Gengsaen]][[zh-min-nan:Keng-sin]][[zh-yue:庚申]][[zh:庚申]]

{{otheruses|Sexagenary cycle|日本人の信仰対象|庚申信仰|qq=}}
{{merge|1=Kōshin|qq=}}
Might be worth moving to or merging with [[Kōshin]]

'''Yang Metal Monkey also known as Kōshin''' (庚申; かのえさる、こうきんのさる、こうしん) is one of the [[Sexagenary cycle]].

It is the 57th combination in the Sexagenary cycle, preceded by [[Yin Earth Goat]] and followed by [[Yin Metal Rooster]]. In [[Yin-Yang and Wuxing Theory]], the stem [[Kanoe (庚)]] of the [[Heavenly Stems|Ten Heavenly Stems]] represents Yang Metal, and the branch [[Monkey (zodiac)|Monkey (申)]] of the [[Earthly Branches|Twelve Earthly Branches]] also represents Yang Metal, a state referred to as [[Wuxing (Chinese philosophy)#Harmonious Relationship (比和)|Harmonious Relationship (Five Elements)]].

==Pronunciation==
* English: Yang Metal Monkey (庚申)
** Chinese (Mandarin Pinyin): gēngshēn
** Cantonese (Jyutping): gaang1 san1
** Japanese:
***Kunyomi 1: Kanoe-saru
***Kunyomi 2: koukin-no-saru
***Onyomi 1: koushin
** Korean (Revised Romanization, Hangul): gyeongsin (경신)
** Vietnamese (Hán-Việt): Canh Thân

== Years of Yang Metal Monkey ==
[[Common Era]] and [[Japanese imperial year|Imperial Era]] designate years that are multiples of 60 as [[Year]] of Kōshin.

=== Nature and Era Change ===
Because both the heavenly stem and earthly branch of Kōshin are of [[Metal]] nature, it was believed that the metal energy pervading the universe in a Kōshin year or day would render human hearts cold and unfeeling. The following combination, [[Yin Metal Rooster]], also being metal in nature—with Xin being of Yin energy—was thought to intensify this coldness. Consequently, Kōshin and Shinyuu were considered harbingers of political upheaval, and to avert such changes it was not uncommon to change eras for two consecutive years. For example, this occurred in the first years of [[Man'en]] ([[1860]]) and [[Bunkyū|Bunkyu]] ([[1861]]).

{| class="wikitable"
|+ '''Kōshin Years'''
|-
! [[1st millennium|1st Millennium]] !! [[2nd millennium|2nd Millennium]] !! [[3rd millennium|3rd Millennium]]
|-
|
* [[AD 60|60 AD]]
* [[AD 120|120 AD]]
* [[180|180 AD]]
* [[240|240 AD]]
* [[300|300 AD]]
* [[AD 360|360 AD]]
* [[AD 420|420 AD]]
* [[480|480 AD]]
* [[540|540 AD]]
* [[600|600 AD]]
* [[660|660 AD]]
* [[720|720 AD]]
* [[780|780 AD]]
* [[840|840 AD]]
* [[900|900 AD]]
* [[960|960 AD]]
|
* [[1020|1020 AD]]
* [[1080|1080 AD]]
* [[1140|1140 AD]]
* [[1200|1200 AD]]
* [[1260|1260 AD]]
* [[1320|1320 AD]]
* [[1380|1380 AD]]
* [[1440|1440 AD]]
* [[1500|1500 AD]]
* [[1560|1560 AD]]
* [[1620|1620 AD]]
* [[1680|1680 AD]]
* [[1740|1740 AD]]
* [[1800|1800 AD]]
* [[1860|1860 AD]]
* [[1920|1920 AD]]
* [[1980|1980 AD]]
|
* [[2040]]
* [[2100s]]
* [[22nd century]]
* [[23rd century]]
* [[23rd century]]
* [[24th century]]
* [[24th century]]
* [[2460 AD]]
* [[3rd millennium]]
* [[2580 AD]]
* [[2640 AD]]
* [[2700 AD]]
* [[2760 AD]]
* [[2820 AD]]
* [[2880 AD]]
* [[2940 AD]]
* [[3rd millennium]]
|}

== Months of Yang Metal Monkey ==
In years where the last digit of the Western Calendar year is 3 or 8 (when the heavenly stem is [[Mizunoto (癸)]] or [[Tsuchinoe (戊)]]), the month of [[July]] becomes the Kōshin [[Month|Month (Calendar)]]. Note that here the month may refer either to a month of the [[Japanese Lunisolar Calendar]] or to a [[Solar term]] (from [[Liqiu|Start of Autumn]] until the day before [[Bailu (solar term)|White Dew]]).

== Days of Yang Metal Monkey ==

On Kōshin days, a Kōshin Vigil ([[Kōshin-machi]], also known as [[Kōshin-machi]]) was held. {{main|[[Kōshin|Kōshin Faith]]|qq=}}

A Kōshin day is also the [[Ennichi|Festival Day]] of [[Śakra (Buddhism)|Taishakuten]].

=== Choosing a Day ===
Kōshin days occur on the sixth day (excluding intercalary days [[Intercalary Day]]) of the [[Hassen]] cycle.
== See Also ==
== External Links ==
* {{Kotobank|2=ブリタニカ国際大百科事典 小項目事典|qq=}}

{{Short description|Folk religion|qq=}}
{{distinguish|Ko-Shintō|qq=}}
{{more footnotes|date=August 2020|qq=}}
{{Italic title|reason=|qq=}}

{{nihongo|'''''Kōshin'''''|庚申|1=Kōshin|lt=Yang Metal Monkey|2=ja|3=庚申|4=zh|5=庚申|6=ko|7=경신|lt_ko=경신|8=fr|9=Singe de métal|10=de|11=Metall-Affe||qq=}} or {{nihongo|''Kōshin-shinkō''|庚申信仰||qq=}} is a folk belief in [[Japan]] with [[Taoism|Taoist]] origins, influenced by [[Shinto]], [[Buddhism]] and other local beliefs.

An event related to the belief is called {{nihongo|Kōshin-kō|庚申講||qq=}}, held on the Kōshin days that occur every 60 days in accordance with the [[:en:Chinese sexagenary cycle|Chinese sexagenary cycle]]. On this day some believers stay awake to prevent {{nihongo|Sanshi|三尸||qq=}}, entities believed to live inside the bodies of believers, from leaving during that night in order to report the deeds of believers to the god Tentei.

It is not known when this belief arrived in Japan, but it was widespread among the nobles of the imperial court by some time in the 9th century. The Japanese monk [[Ennin]], visiting [[Tang dynasty|Tang China]] in 838, wrote that "This night no one sleeps. It is the same as New Year's Eve and the nights of ''Kōshin'' in our country."<ref>{{Cite book |last= |first= |url=http://archive.org/details/diaryrecordofpil0000enni |title=Ennin's Diary. The Record of a Pilgrimage to China in Search of the Law |title-link= |publisher=Ronald Press Company |year=1955 |location=New York |page=58 |translator-last=Reischauer |translator-first=Edwin O. |translator-link=Edwin O. Reischauer|qq=}}</ref> In the [[Muromachi period]], [[Bhikkhu|Buddhist monks]] started to write about Kōshin and spread the belief to a wider public. Numerous monuments or pillars called {{nihongo|Kōshin-tō|庚申塔||qq=}} (or also {{nihongo|Kōshin-zuka|庚申塚||qq=}}) were erected all over the country, and the belief remained very popular during the [[Edo period]]. However, after the Meiji government issued the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]] in 1872, Kōshin and other folk beliefs were officially rejected as superstitions.

Belief in Kōshin survives, although it is far less popular than it once was. Many Kōshin-tō have been moved inside Buddhist temples or private homes, but many remain alongside roads and there are still some well-maintained {{nihongo|Kōshin-dō|Kōshin-dō||qq=}} (Kōshin halls) either attached to Buddhist temples or standing alone.

==Deities and customs==
The most ancient Kōshin custom is that of staying awake one special night every sixty days. This night is called ''Kōshin-Machi'' (庚申待 - Kōshin Waiting).

The main Kōshin belief is the concept that [[Three Corpses]], or ''Sanshi'' (三尸), live in every human body. The Sanshi keep track of the deeds of the person they inhabit. On the night called Kōshin-Machi, the ''Sanshis'' leave the body and go to [[:en:King of Heavens|Tentei]] (天帝), the Heavenly God, to report the deeds of that person. Tentei then punishes bad people, making them ill, shortening their lifespans or, in extreme cases, ending their lives. Believers in Kōshin try to live without performing bad deeds, but those who believe that they have reason to fear try to stay awake during Kōshin nights, as the only way to prevent the Sanshi from reporting to Tentei.

In the [[Edo period]], Kōshin-Machi became more popular among commoners, leading to a proliferation of festivities and cultic activities. Many of these practices centered on the worship of specific deities. These deities were visualized in [[Hanging scroll]]s, icons, and stone carvings, many of which continue to dot the rural landscape of Japan today. One specially prominent god in the Kōshin cult was [[Shōmen-Kongō]] (Blue-Faced [[:en:Vajra-Yakṣa|Vajra-Yakṣa]]), a fearsome deity with many arms. The first scriptures that established his connection with Kōshin were produced in the context of esoteric Buddhist rituals performed at the temple known as {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Onjōji|Onjōji]]|qq=}} or {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Miidera|Miidera]]|qq=}}. Another temple crucial in the early development of this cult was [[Shitennō-ji]]. Shōmen-Kongō became Kōshin-san when people expected this deity to make the Sanshis themselves ill and prevent them from going to Tentei.

==See also==
* [[Yasaka Kōshin-dō]]

==References and further reading==
{{reflist|qq=}}
* Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney, ''The Monkey as Mirror: Symbolic Transformations in Japanese History and Ritual (Asian Studies/Anthropology)'', Princeton University Press, 1989, {{ISBN|978-0-691-02846-0|qq=}}
* Kubo, Noritada (窪 徳忠) ''Kōshin Shinkō no Kenkyū: Nitchū Shūkyō Bunka Kōshō-shi (庚申信仰の研究―日中宗教文化交渉史)'' (Research on Belief in Kōshin - A History of Cultural-Religious Exchange, Nihon Gakujutsu Shinkō-kai, 1961.
* Emiko Ohnuki-Tierney, ''Illness and Culture in Contemporary Japan: An Anthropological View'', Cambridge University Press, 1984, {{ISBN|978-0-521-27786-0|qq=}}
* Lafcadio Hearn, ''Japan's Religions: Shinto and Buddhism'', Kessinger Publishing Co, 2003, {{ISBN|978-0-7661-7657-7|qq=}} 
* Ichiro Hori, ''Folk Religion in Japan: Continuity and Change'', University of Chicago Press, 1974, {{ISBN|978-0-226-35334-0|qq=}}
* Livia Kohn, ''Daoism and Chinese Culture'', Three Pines Press, 2005, {{ISBN|978-1-931483-00-1|qq=}}
* [[Richard Bowring]], ''The Religious Traditions of Japan'', Cambridge University Press, 2005, {{ISBN|978-0-521-85119-0|qq=}}
* [http://www.three-monkeys.info/1/ENG.htm Three-Monkeys site with related research and information]

== External links ==
*[https://web.archive.org/web/20120316111118/http://www.irohaproject.org/blog/2010/03/09/koushin-1/ The koshin belief, The Iroha Project]

{{Shinto shrine|qq=}}

{{Hiyoshi Faith|qq=}}

[[File:koshinscroll.jpg|thumb|A Kōshin scroll|right]]

[[fa:کوشین شینکو]]

{{wikidata link|Q798775}}
[[Category:Japanese folk religion]]
[[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]
[[Category:Kōshin]]
[[Category:Kōshin| ]]
[[Category:Regions of Asia]]
[[Category:Regions of Japan]]
[[Category:Religious towers]]
[[Category:Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō]]
[[Category:Sexagenary cycle members]]
[[Category:Git synced pages]]
[[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]]
